differential_diagnoses: Insulin receptor antibody syndrome (Type B insulin resistance)
Severe insulin resistance due to rare genetic variants (e.g., PPARG or AKT2 mutations)
next_steps: Test for anti-insulin receptor antibodies (Type B insulin resistance)
Quantify insulin-binding antibodies (to rule out insulin autoimmune syndrome)
Genetic testing for lipodystrophy-associated genes (e.g., PLIN1, CIDEC, PPARG)
Repeat leptin level confirmation and check adiponectin levels (to differentiate subtypes)
Comprehensive metabolic panel and lipid profile monitoring
Monitor for complications of severe lipodystrophy such as cardiomyopathy and renal disease
Consultation with endocrinology and immunology specialists for potential immunosuppressive therapy

differential_diagnoses: Type B insulin resistance syndrome (insulin receptor antibody syndrome)
Acquired generalized lipodystrophy (AGL) associated with autoimmune/connective tissue disease
Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) manifesting with autoantibodies and insulin resistance
Paraneoplastic syndrome associated with occult splenic or lymphoproliferative malignancy
Severe insulin resistance secondary to other autoantibody-mediated syndromes
next_steps: Test for anti-insulin receptor antibodies (functional assay to distinguish agonist/antagonist activity)
Biopsy and histopathological examination of the splenic lesion
Comprehensive autoimmune panel including anti-ENA, anti-dsDNA, C3/C4 levels, and anti-phospholipid antibodies
Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy review (evaluate for hematologic malignancy or underlying marrow disorder)
Consultation with immunology for consideration of immunosuppressive therapies (e.g., rituximab, cyclophosphamide, or corticosteroids)
Continue glycemic management with U-500 insulin while assessing for autoimmune markers
Screening for underlying malignancy with PET-CT
